data.title: Broadcom Continues To Take Nvidia's Lunch
data.description:
Broadcom (NASDAQ: NASDAQ:AVGO) is already the company we mentioned as part of the next AI wave. Broadcom performed well after hours after the announcement during earnings that the company had a new unnamed $10 billion XPU customer. As we'll see throughout this article, the opportunity in AI isn't in making the shovels and selling it to the miners, it's in helping miners make their own shovels.

Broadcom's central positioning there enables it to drive strong shareholder returns.

XPUs and Broadcom

One of Nvidia's largest risks which we've discussed is that it has massively concentrated revenue.

The risk of this concentration is that the company's customers are massive tech companies. They're companies with substantial resources and assets, assets that we expect them to use to work around Nvidia. In our analogy above, Nvidia is the company making the shovels for miners. Broadcom is the company that's helping miners make their own shovels.

The massive scale of datacenters means that companies can afford to spend to optimize each and every part of the process. Broadcom stands to benefit substantially from that. The fact that Broadcom acquired a 4th customer after its existing 3 XPU customers in a $10 billion deal, shows the scale of opportunity available to Broadcom.

For perspective, Broadcom's first 3 customers are expected to reach 1 million XPUs, which means the potential of $10s of billion per customer in revenue.

Broadcom 3Q Results

Broadcom had strong 3Q results showing the company's continued portfolio strength.

The company saw 22% non-GAAP revenue growth to almost $16 billion for the quarter. That puts the company at more than $60 billion in annual revenue, making it an incredibly large and diversified tech company, with impressive growth. The company managed to achieve even more impressive cash flow growth with more than $7 billion in FCF.

That has pushed the company's annualized FCF to more than $28 billion, putting the company at a 2% FCF yield. While, it's a FCF level that requires growth, the company has a more than 20% growth rate, showing its strength.

Broadcom Diversified Positioning

Broadcom has built up one of the few tech conglomerates with an impressive diversified portfolio.

The company is guiding for more than $50 billion in FY24 revenue, with almost $10 billion in R&D spending. The company's continued investments along with acquisitions have built it a diversified portfolio with ~20K patents. Broadcom's diversified portfolio here makes it incredibly difficult for another company to enter its world.

More importantly, while Nvidia's GPUs can be replicated by XPUs, something already done by Google's TPUs etc., no company has managed to replicate Broadcom's advanced packaging or TSMC's fabs as these technical industries have much higher barriers to entry.

We think the complicated technical nature of XPUs means the barrier to replace Nvidia's GPUs with XPUs is dramatically lower than the barrier to replace Broadcom's packaging.

Shareholder Returns

At the end of the day, what matters is shareholder returns, and Broadcom remains a profitable company.

YoY the company improved its FCF by almost 50%, showing its financial strength. The company is at a 2% FCF yield but with 20% revenue growth YoY and continued expense management, the company can quickly grow to a FCF yield that justifies its valuation. More importantly, the company has yet to realize the benefits of the XPU business.

A lot of the scale that the company has talked about is expected to come in the 2026-2027 time period, into the end of the decade. That means that the company's revenue and FCF growth strength is likely to continue.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is the company's success is predicated on continued substantial AI demand. Even if Broadcom succeeds in taking away Nvidia's lunch, major companies still need to be spending $10s of billions on AI for Broadcom to be earning the amount of funding to drive substantial returns at Broadcom's valuation.

Conclusion

Broadcom has an impressive portfolio of technological assets, and the company just reported strong earnings helping its share price increase by double-digits. The company is incredibly well positioned to take GPU business from Nvidia as the major AI companies can afford to invest at scale in having their own custom silicon.

That strength is shown through the company's win of another $10 billion XPU customer. We expect this business for the company to scale dramatically in the next few years, providing substantial revenue and FCF, and enabling Broadcom to continue to grow its shareholder returns. Putting all of this together, Broadcom is an interesting investment opportunity.

data.title: Timber ETFs: WOOD's Growth Potential Vs. CUT's Defensive Position
data.description:
The timber ETF investing landscape is composed of two ETFs: the iShares Global Timber & Forestry ETF (NASDAQ:WOOD) and the Invesco MSCI Global Timber ETF (NYSEARCA:CUT). I give both of them a hold rating.

Of course, both ETFs will allow you to capture upside thanks to strong global trends of sustainability, decarbonization, and urbanization but the structure of the market caps the upside you can expect. In that regard, thanks to its exposure to emerging and smaller capitalizations, I believe WOOD is the best option.

The Timber Sector’s Recovery

I mentioned sustainability as a key trend, and I believe this is the main interest of timber equities. I have net zero commitments in mind for many countries with a goal in 2050 but (and this is a big but) it was true under the Biden administration, it is not really the case anymore with Trump. The trend remains sound in Europe, and is also gaining momentum in Asia, with delayed targets (2060 to 2070).

The sawlog and wood panel consumption is projected to increase at a CAGR of 3.7% until 2050, driven by innovations like engineered timbers such as cross-laminated timber (CLT) or glulam that can be used as construction materials instead of steel and concrete, resulting in a reduced carbon footprint of up to 35%. This is still a niche market as despite the environmental benefits it still costs 21% more for a standard house in the US, meaning that the adoption will not increase until ways of cutting costs are found. This means that for now, holdings like Weyerhaeuser (WY) and Svenska Cellulosa (OTCPK:SVCBF), found in both portfolio main holdings at around 8% to 10% of the total won’t see a significant upside. The UK has committed to support wood-based constructions, and WOOD has a 5% exposure that CUT doesn’t have.

Portfolios

WOOD is tracking the S&P Global Timber & Forestry Index with a portfolio of 26 companies established in 11 countries. The main focus is on process industries (70.49% of the fund), including major US actors like Weyerhaeuser (WY) and PotlatchDeltic (PCH) or their Brazilian counterpart Suzano (SUZ). The fund is also exposed to Asian markets with Japan (13%) and China (8.56%). The fund has a high top 10 concentration of 54.37% that introduces a high level of risks like wildfire or pest outbreaks. The fund has a focus on emerging actors that could capture higher growth with a 57.57% allocation to mid-caps, a 19.04% allocation to small-caps and only 17.04% to large-caps.

CUT is tracking the MSCI ACWI IMI Timber Select Capped Index with a portfolio of 57 companies and a 48.67% top 10 concentration. As I mentioned in the introduction, this fund is less exposed to emerging markets and leans more into the US (49%), Finland (12%) and Sweden (8%) with a small 1.2% China allocation; the fund is also reliant on process industries (77.36%) and is exposed to REITs at 9.17%, something that differentiates the fund with WOOD. The goal of this constituent is to provide a buffer against price volatility of lumber, but I think it further caps an upside that is already not fantastic. Reflective on its more conservative geographical split, CUT also relies more on well established companies with a 31.76% allocation to large-caps and 54.71% to mid-caps. Small-caps only amount to 11.84% of the portfolio against 19.04% for WOOD.

WOOD is more directly exposed to the demand for lumber while CUT relies more on processing activities. Based on both portfolios, I estimate a roughly 55/45 split for WOOD between direct demand for lumber and indirect demand with processed products and 45/55 for CUT.

Both funds are passive, so differences are coming from their index methodology. The reference index of WOOD is using a market-cap-weighted methodology that gives higher weights to companies with large equity floats while ensuring an exposure to emerging markets (Brazil / East-Asia) that are regions with higher timber demand and price sensitivity. This is a sound strategy as I don’t see that much upside for now in developed markets, but given the 20% exposure to APAC, tariffs in place are adding to the volatility of markets already known to be volatile. I do think that this is an opportunity for a price dip, that could in turn justify a buy rating. For now, the situation is just too tense. On the other hand, CUT’s index has a different methodology. It also follows a market-cap weighted approach but with a 5% cap on single holdings to limit concentration. However, the market is either already consolidated, or consolidating (like in Europe) and I don’t fully agree with that 5% cap. It can be appealing as it forces to get exposure beyond timber and forestry firms, and this is why CUT is holding REITs that WOOD is not holding, and this results in a more defensive portfolio. A key difference is the rebalancing frequency: quarterly for CUT and annually for WOOD. The case for quarterly rebalancing is to avoid ETF drift vs. the market, while annual rebalancing results in 4x less trades and lower transaction fees. Given the low returns of both funds, I like WOOD’s approach more because I don’t mind paying fees for good reasons, but paying fees to a strategy with low returns is something I will never do (returns are in the next part).

Financial Metrics

WOOD’s expense ratio is much lower than CUT’s: 0.40% and 0.82%. WOOD also manages much more assets: the fund’s AUM is $251.4 Mn, nearly 5x more than CUT’s $46.6 Mn. Same goes for the liquidity, WOOD is better than CUT but both are red flags: the daily trading volume of WOOD is 0.2% of its AUM and that of CUT is 0.4% of its AUM. This results in a high 30 Day Median Bid/Ask Spread for both: 0.41% for WOOD and 0.53% for CUT. There really is a liquidity problem in this industry.

WOOD’s dividend yield is low, 1.43% despite its high 30-day SEC yield of 3.30%. I think it is fair to assume that the dividend will soon be increased. CUT’s dividend ratio has the opposite dynamic 3.11% for a 30-day SEC yield of 2.32%. Turning to valuations, WOOD is a lot less expensive than CUT with a P/E ratio of 11.76 and a P/B ratio of 0.94, against 18.22 and 1.30 for CUT.

So far, WOOD appears to be a clear winner, let’s look at the performance:

Both funds are close, WOOD appears to be more volatile despite lower valuation metrics. Overall, historical performances are a disaster, with no value creation over the last 3 years, even destruction for CUT. The short term returns are abysmal and long term returns are barely above 5%.

Since July, both funds have increased: +4.8% for WOOD and +3.6% for CUT. This is a high figure, and not the kind of volatility you would expect from timber stocks in normal times. This is an additional reason why I think that now is not the best way to invest, and thus recommend a hold.

Market Dynamics

The long term demand for timber is supported by several trends, such as urbanization and decarbonization.

The demand for timber is also exposed to interest rates because of the construction sector (that I estimate to account for nearly 60% of the demand for wood), so on this side the outlook is positive. For example in the US housing starts in 2025 were low at 1.3% and the projection for 2026 is 8.6%. The demand in Asia might not recover as fast, driven down by the housing market in China. A good way to monitor trends in Asia can be done by looking at the company Nine Dragons Paper (not in our portfolios) as a proxy for price and volume trends.

In short, in 2026, once the US housing market has picked up and Nine Dragons Paper’s results are pointing in the right direction, it would be a signal to buy WOOD and its 20% exposure to Asia.

The push to paper packaging is fueling the need for timber with a global market projected to grow at a 4.8% CAGR until 2030. In early 2024, the estimated figure was 3 Bn trees per year dedicated to paper packaging, I estimate it to be around 30% of the timber industry, and this figure will grow as the CAGR for the paper packaging industry is above the figure I gave you earlier for the increase of global demand for wood. Two types of woods are used for the packaging industries: softwoods (pine, spruce, fir, and hemlock) and hardwoods (eucalyptus, birch, and aspen).

If we look at our ETFs, they both are significantly exposed to these types of woods, but WOOD slightly more than CUT (50% for CUT vs. 55% for WOOD I’d say). Wood holds companies operating in the pulp, paper, and packaging production such as Weyerhaeuser, Svenska Cellulosa, and Suzano while CUT also includes companies like International Paper (IP), Packaging Corp of America (PKG), WestRock, Smurfit Kappa, and UPM-Kymmene (OTCPK:UPMKF) but with smaller weights.

Risks

One thing that also doesn’t make me want to buy now is the significant risk level, outweighing risks.

I mentioned liquidity earlier, this is an issue for both funds with huge bid/ask spreads: 0.41% for WOOD and 0.53% for CUT.

Both portfolios are significantly concentrated in the top 10, even CUT and its 5% cap, and this exposes both funds to the performance of key holdings and introduces specific risks such as wildfires, pest outbreaks or even regulations. Tariffs are also a risk, and are introducing a high level of price volatility given the uncertainty about future evolutions.

Both funds are subject to interest rate sensitivity, for reasons about the housing market I evoked before, but also because of their exposure to mainly non large-cap companies that are facing tense borrowing conditions and that could ease with the cuts, allowing for aggressive growth in a best case scenario where rate cuts are boosting wood demand by stimulating the housing market and the private consumption, small actors would benefit from favorable financing markets. This is obviously partly integrated in prices today, and a delay in the rate cuts would make both ETF prices tank (which could turn into a buy opportunity).

As both ETFs are exposed internationally, 50% for CUT and 70% for WOOD, a significant part of the returns are not denominated in dollars and the returns are dependent on the dollar index. This is not necessarily a bad thing, as the outlook for the dollar is not the best and UBS recommends holding foreign currencies.

Conclusion

If you want to invest in the timber market with ETFs, you don’t have many options, you will either buy WOOD or CUT. Right now, none of them are a hold because the upside potential is not enough considering the risks, but this could change after a market correction or a change in the market structure.

When that happens, I recommend you pick WOOD as it is positioned more aggressively with smaller companies and in emerging markets, while CUT is more conservative, exposed to markets that are already mature and where I don’t see enough upside.

data.title: What Moved Markets This Week
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Wall Street ended the holiday-shortened week mixed as hopes of more aggressive Fed easing this year were balanced with concerns about a recession as the weakness in the labor market was illustrated in sharp relief.

The S&P 500 (SP500) closed up +0.3% for the week, with the Dow (DJI) down -0.3% and the Nasdaq (COMP.IND) rallying by +1.1%.

On Friday, the August jobs report showed an anemic 22K rise in nonfarm payrolls last month, while revisions showed the economy actually lost jobs in June. The markets fully priced in a September Fed rate cut of 25 bps, with a 10% chance the FOMC goes big with 50 bps.

A global bond selloff that started the week reversed by the end. In the Treasury market, fears about the speed of labor market deterioration pushed the 10-year yield (US10Y) to a 5-month low, as it fell more than 15 bps on the week to end at 4.07%.

Gold (CUR:XAUUSD) benefited from the drop in rates, powering above $3,500/oz. and gaining +4% for the week.

The Nasdaq got a boost from Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), which saw its largest non-earnings gap higher open since 2006 after an antitrust court decided it did not have to divest its Chrome browser and could still charge for search inclusion. Analysts called this a major plus and the stock finished up +10.1% on the week.

Also on the corporate front, PepsiCo (PEP) ended the week down -1.5% despite activist Elliott management taking a $4B stake in the company.

Read a preview of next week's major events in Seeking Alpha's Catalyst Watch.

Seeking Alpha News Quiz

Get ready for a challenge every week! Test your knowledge on the most recent happenings in the investing world by taking the new Seeking Alpha News Quiz and seeing how you stack up against the competition.

Seeking Alpha's Calls Of The Week

Royal Caribbean Cruises: I'm Not Ready For This Cruise

AMD: Left In Nvidia's Dust As Valuations Reach Excessive Levels

Micron: Time To Jump Back On The AI Bullet Train

Intel: Some Hope Is Better Than None

Build-A-Bear Q2 Review: An Amazing Turnaround Story

GE Aerospace: The Stunning Rally Is Likely To Pause

Dick's Sporting Goods: Quick Bounce Returns Shares To Fair Value

Celestica: Forward P/E Mirrors Nvidia

Institutional Investors And A Contrarian Look At CNX Resources

Marvell: Robust Data Center Market Drives 25%+ Revenue Growth

Weekly Movement

U.S. Indices
Dow -0.3% to 45,401. S&P 500 +0.3% to 6,482. Nasdaq +1.1% to 21,700. Russell 2000 +1% to 2,391. CBOE Volatility Index -1.2% to 15.18.

S&P 500 Sectors
Consumer Staples +0.3%. Utilities -1.1%. Financials -1.7%. Telecom +5.1%. Healthcare +0.4%. Industrials -0.9%. Information Technology +0.2%. Materials -0.4%. Energy -3.5%. Consumer Discretionary +1.6%. Real Estate -0.3%.

World Indices
London +0.2% to 9,208. France -0.4% to 7,675. Germany -1.3% to 23,597. Japan +0.7% to 43,019. China -1.2% to 3,813. Hong Kong +1.4% to 25,418. India +1.1% to 80,711.

Commodities and Bonds
Crude Oil WTI -3.3% to $61.87/bbl. Gold +3.9% to $3,653.3/oz. Natural Gas +1.7% to 3.048. Ten-Year Bond Yield -0.2 bps to 4.086.

Forex and Cryptos
EUR/USD +0.28%. USD/JPY +0.24%. GBP/USD +0.04%. Bitcoin +1.9%. Litecoin +1.2%. Ethereum -1.2%. XRP +0.2%.

Top S&P 500 Gainers
Autodesk (ADSK) +13%. Western Digital (WDC) +12%. Alphabet (GOOG) +11%. Seagate Technology Holdings plc (STX) +11%. Broadcom (AVGO) +9%.

Top S&P 500 Losers
lululemon athletica (LULU) -18%. Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) -10%. Dollar Tree (DLTR) -10%. Kenvue (KVUE) -9%. GE Vernova (GEV) -8%.

Where will the markets be headed next week? Current trends and ideas? Add your thoughts to the comments section.

data.title: Microsoft: No Signs Of Slowing Down
data.description:
Investment thesis

My previous bullish article about Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT) aged well because the stock's total return outpaced the broader market's growth since then. The company's fundamentals remain ironclad, and I see several bullish recent developments. Despite its fundamental strength, valuation ratios suggest that Microsoft is reasonably valued. All in all, I keep accumulating my MSFT position and reiterate a "Strong Buy" rating.

Recent developments

I can see that the very positive recent results certainly tell a good story. FQ4 results released on July 30th 2025, saw consolidated revenue growth increase to 18.1% YoY, with revenue hitting $76.44 billion vs. $73.84 billion expected by consensus.

Quarterly EPS surged from $2.95 to $3.65 YoY vs. $3.38 expected by consensus. Of interest to investors is that Microsoft's annual revenue from its flagship Azure cloud solution, exceeded $75 billion for fiscal 2025. Upcoming EPS for fiscal Q1 is expected to be $3.66 on revenues of $75.45 billion. That will mean a 15% YoY revenue growth and an 11% EPS increase. As shown in the following table, there were significantly more upward EPS revisions than downgrades over the last 90 days.

Such optimism around Microsoft's next quarterly earnings release is because of the company's fundamental strength. MSFT is strategically positioning itself right at the heart of major shifts in technological trends including the adoption of AI across its range of services as well as engaging with major partners to expand its footprint.

As a solid example, Microsoft recently announced a new partnership with Samsung to include its Copilot AI assistant in Samsung’s new range of Smart Monitors and AI-powered TVs. With Samsung seeing demand for larger models, it will mean that owners will be able to communicate directly with their flagship screen using either the remote control or the built in voice command technology for an intuitive experience. As well as hardware integration, Copilot’s enhanced AI will also be available on Samsung’s lifestyle hub, Daily+.

Another partnership with vibe coding start-up, Replit, that was also recently announced, will see Replit’s software creation offerings become available on Azure Marketplace and Microsoft’s cloud services. As well, Microsoft has partnered with the NFL to integrate Microsoft’s AI business solutions into its gameplay. The partnership has ensured that the NFL's Sideline Viewing System for every club has been upgraded. 32 clubs, encompassing 1,000 coaches and 1,800 players, have employed more than 2,500 Microsoft Surface Copilot+ PCs to provide real-time game data and analysis for over 330 events annually. The partnership looks really promising because the "AI in Sports" niche is expected to observe a quite aggressive 21.1% CAGR by 2030.

Microsoft’s close affiliation with Open AI is also, in my opinion, underrated. This alliance is right at the forefront of the secular shift. The company is closely collaborating with Open AI for voice agents and speech-to-speech models, amongst many other aspects of AI. This is also very promising collaboration because experts believe that global voice AI market has potential to demonstrate exponential growth over the next decade.

Of course, I cannot ignore the fact that the company, whilst on the cutting edge of the most exciting technology in decades, also has a rock-solid financial position. This means that to investors, it represents a safe inroad into new technologies. A look at its balance sheet will reassure any investor. With nearly $95 billion of cash on hand the company is well positioned to swoop in on any acquisitions that will go on to further solidify its position in AI space.

There is overwhelming consensus that the AI cycle is still in the early stages. Nvidia’s results helped to quell any doubts about that. The very same reason that sees Microsoft as the less obvious AI trade may indeed lead to its share price jumping higher as it continues to be a leader in the sector.

Valuation update

With a market cap of approximately $3.8 trillion, Microsoft has maintained its valuation ratios very close to its 5-year average, yet the majority of these have not included the full potential of the company’s AI offerings. Thus future earnings potential is robust and forecasted P/E compression will be due to enhanced earnings. Consensus by Wall Street analysts is for a 20%+ upside potential.

With lower interest rates in the U.S. predicted to be coming to the fore in the latter part of 2025 and throughout 2026, the backdrop is favorable. Within the next 12 months the scenario for interest rates is expected to be much more positive for the overall stock market and for the continued valuation expansion of Microsoft.

Risks update

With such a bullish backdrop and forecast, are there any risks to buying and/or holding Microsoft currently? Well, I can identify the following, but both are likely to be short-lived.

Firstly, there are regulatory considerations especially in markets outside of the U.S., with Europe firmly in the crosshairs here. Privacy and online safety laws continue to evolve, and nimble compliance will be necessary on an on-going basis. That said, the Trump administration has the protection of digital providers high on its list and it is noted that some of the reasons for many of the recent tariffs have referenced unhappiness about international digital taxes and compliance issues.

Secondly there is the so-called “September Lull”. September and February are considered to be the two worst performing months historically for the stock market as a whole and also for Microsoft. Yet, this does not translate into a negative performance. It might just be considered that it is potentially a less positive one or that there may be a slightly higher chance of a pullback. Of note is that August is also considered a less positive month, but the major U.S. indices all put in stellar performances this August. Which just goes to show that markets can buck the trend very easily!

Bottom line

Overall, I find it hard to come to any other conclusion that the many positive aspects of owning Microsoft outweigh any potential pitfalls and to this extent it remains a top pick as a solid blue-chip investment.

data.title: Norbit: Raised Guidance, Raised Expectations, Delivery Is Everything Going Forward
data.description:
Introduction

Norbit ASA (OTCPK:NBITF) is up more than 100% YTD, with strong growth and earnings momentum. Norbit has caught my attention by creating great returns for shareholders over several years, with support from top and bottom line growth. In this article I will go through the latest two quarters, and take a look at the outlook and guidance, and go through Norbit's valuation and what their current risks are. Now over to a short introduction about Norbit ASA.

Norbit is a technology firm that has specialized in sonars used for the marine market. The technology is used primarily for inspection and control of underwater environments and 3D picturing. However, Norbit has expended their usage and now can be used for surveillance, digital signal processing and high-resolution data. The largest operations are in North America and Europe, with headquarters in Trondheim, Norway.

What has happened?

Norbit has seen an astonishing return the past few years. Norbit is up 626% the past three years, and up 110% YTD on the Norwegian stock exchange, OSBEX. However, their momentum has paused the past three months, showing signs of investors being more cautions and that the price is near its short-term limit. I will look at their current valuation more closely in the valuation part of my article.

Now over to the financials. Norbit has managed to steadily grow top-line since 2017 except for 2020. This is shows that Norbit has been quite successful with scaling their business. Additionally, Norbit has been quite consistent with growing bottom-line. These are the exact signs I am looking for when screening for new investing ideas.

In Q2, Norbit reached a new revenue quarter record of NOK 684 million up 63% YoY, and up 31.2% QoQ. Substantial growth both YoY and just quarter over quarter. Norbit's group gross margin stands at 55% last quarter, down 7% QoQ due to product mix. PIR is Norbit's least profitable segment, and it now represents a bigger percentage of group revenue, from 31% to 43% just QoQ. PIR gross margin additionally fell due to higher share of high-volume contract manufacturing production within the segment, which can be explained as raw material products with a markup. EBIT was up approximately 70% YoY and up 36.7% QoQ. Their EBIT margin now stands at 25% in H1 2025, which is a new high for half a year. On a quarter basis, EBIT margin is up 10 bps YoY. Net income is up 82% YoY and up 46.5% QoQ, this really shows the immense growth Norbit has experienced the past year.

Metric Q1 2025 Q2 2025 QoQ Δ
Group revenue (NOK m) 521.7 684.4 +31.2%
Group gross margin 62% 55% –7 pp
Group EBITDA (NOK m) 162.0 210.7 +30%
Group EBIT (NOK m) 127.4 174.2 +36.7%
Net income (NOK m) 89.7 131.4 +46.5%
Oceans revenue (NOK m) 232.7 239.4 +2.9%
Oceans EBIT (NOK m / %) 81.4 / 35% 86.7 / 36% +6.5% / +1 pp
Connectivity revenue (NOK m) 145.9 169.8 +16.4%
Connectivity EBIT (NOK m / %) 41.5 / 28% 55.2 / 32% +33.0% / +4 pp
PIR revenue (NOK m) 160.6 293.1 +82.5%
PIR EBIT (NOK m / %) 21.8 / 14% 59.6 / 20% +173.4% /+6 pp

Oceans are Norbit's biggest segment, they offer ultra-compact multibeam and side-scan sonars for a range of special applications, including seabed mapping and hydrography, as well as sub-bottom profilers for subsurface imaging. Oceans saw a 22% YoY revenue increase, where half of this came from acquisition of Innomar, so organic growth was 11%. Oceans only saw a modest 2.9% growth QoQ. APAC was the main contributor to sales growth this quarter, with both Europe and Americas posting a small decline in sales YoY. Oceans EBIT margin stands at 36%, down from 41% last year due to consolidation from Innomar, wage inflation up 46% compared to revenue growth of 22%.

Connectivity segment is their leading technology solution for asset identification, monitoring and tracking. This segment saw 67% growth YoY this quarter.

Revenues primarily increased on sale of On-Board units and sale of enforcement modules for tachographs as a result of the EU requirement to replace all non-smart analogue or digital tachographs.

The demand for these On-Boards units is visible just QoQ with 16% growth. EBIT margin currently stands at 32% up from 20% last year, and up 4% QoQ. The margin expansion comes from significant sales growth compared to operating costs, this shows that Norbit has operating leverage, which means that Norbit is able to increase EBIT margin substantially because of semi fixed costs. Therefore, there is a possibility that Norbit could rise EBIT margin further.

The Product Innovation & Realization also called PIR offers R&D services and

contract manufacturing to long-term key industrial customers through in-house capabilities and with a high degree of robotised production. Their PIR segment has seen outstanding growth, up more than 114% YoY and up 82.5% QoQ. Regardless of this growth, revenues actually fell 15% short of expectations due to supply chain issues, which will instead take delivery in Q3. The main event for the quarter is European client in the defence and security sector. The contract value of NOK 125 million to be delivered in Q4. EBIT margin is at 20% up from 13% last year and 6% QoQ. PIR has strong operating leverage as well, as employee costs and other expenses are stabile in the short-term. Therefore, as revenue is expected to increase, I will believe that EBIT margin could increase towards the end of the year.

Next quarter coming up is Q3. It is set to release 13 Nov. Q3 is usually a lot softer quarter for Norbit because of seasonality, therefore it is expected to fall QoQ.

However, looking YoY instead, it is clear that it seems like Norbit's growth will still continue strong, with overall growth of 38% if they hit mid target.

Guidance for the full year is raised to NOK 2.5-26 billion in 2025 with an EBIT margin of 25%. There is one analyst covering Norbit which is important to note, and the analyst's revenue estimate is equal to the company guidance, but as time gets the analysts expects slower growth further into the future, but with an average CAGR the next four years of 14%.

Valuation and momentum

In my valuation, I use a discounted EPS valuation. It uses three different scenarios based on different growth rates and multiples. All the scenarios use an EPS of 6.62, and a discount rate of 10% which is the discount rate I use for all my valuations.

The first scenario is the base case, it uses a growth rate of 10% the first five, and 5% the last five. This scenario showcases what type of valuation Norbit will have if they grow low double to mid single digits, and achieve a multiple of 32x. This scenario calculates a share price of NOK 240 or $24.

The second scenario is the best case, it uses a growth rate of 12% the first five, and then 10% the last five years. This scenario is based on a stable double-digit growth, which is more inline with the current growth, it also uses a terminal multiple of 30x, which is significantly below the current multiple of 50x. This scenario calculates a share price of NOK 314 or $31.4.

The third scenario uses a growth rate of 5%, well below the current growth. This scenario showcases what type of valuation Norbit could have if growth reaches a steady rate of 5%, with a lower multiple of 25x. It calculates a share price of NOK 162 or $16, which is 20% below the current valuation. This tells me that Norbit current valuation is very conservative.

Risks

Norbit currently has a debt to equity ratio of 37%. It has slightly increased from 11% five years ago. However, their ICR is still solid at 13.7x, which comes from continued improved earnings.

PIR segment can also become a concern as this segment is experience a lot of growth, it could dilute Norbit's total profitability as PIR segment has an EBIT margin significantly below their other two segments.

Norbit's biggest risk is currently tied up to their high valuation and growth rate, and with that they need to continue to deliver strong results to keep the momentum up. Norbit is also a relatively small company that comes with different risks as execution risk, operational risk and segment risk.

Conclusion

Norbit has performed remarkably in the past years, with strong demand and earnings momentum. They continue to deliver great results, but due to high multiples they have to keep delivering to keep the momentum upwards and maintain the current valuation. Norbit's has shown that they are able to increase both top-line and margins with strong operating leverage. My discounted EPS valuation shows that despite a FWD PE ratio of 35x, there is still a significant upside potential. Therefore, due to strong momentum and upside potential, I rate Norbit ASA a buy!

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Krispy Kreme: Growth Story Gets Fundamentally Challenged (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Summary

Following my coverage on Krispy Kreme (NASDAQ:DNUT) in April ’25, in which I recommended a buy rating as the business fundamentals were very well intact, this post is to provide an update on my thoughts on the business and stock. Fast forward to 2Q25, my bull thesis has collapsed. The termination of the McDonald’s deal, coupled with massive impairment charges and a failing DFD strategy, has fundamentally changed the growth narrative. The balance sheet is also now heavily strained, leaving little margin for error. I now double downgrade to sell.

The McDonald's Partnership Termination

When I last wrote about DNUT in April, the McDonald’s partnership looked like a game-changer and was the crux of my bull thesis. The plan to scale rapidly into thousands of doors was promising because it was a capital-light, high-visibility path to growth that, at least on paper, would solve DNUT’s biggest bottleneck—distribution. Fast forward to today, this important piece of the puzzle has not just weakened but completely wiped away. The termination of the partnership agreement was a fundamental blow to the DNUT growth story. Overnight, DNUT effectively lost ~2,400 delivered fresh daily [DFD] doors.

DNUT’s own reasoning for the breakup was that the partnership was unsustainable because operating costs far exceeded the unit demand that McDonald’s locations could generate. This was very surprising because I’d imagine a lot of planning and trial runs must have been done before rolling out this strategy. To learn that the unit economics simply don’t work at this stage really shows how poor planning/execution has been. My belief is that donuts are, by nature, a low-price, high-frequency product, and I think this failure highlights just how difficult it is to make the math work when distribution expands faster than demand. The financial impact was not small. Management estimates the McDonald’s deal inflicted an adj. EBITDA impact of $12 million to $15 million in just 1H25, with $7 million to $9 million of that drag hitting the US segment in 2Q25 alone. Put simply, the most high-profile partnership in DNUT’s history ended up accelerating losses rather than driving profitable growth.

This failure directly impacted DNUT’s balance sheet. For perspective, in 2Q25, DNUT recorded a staggering $406.9 million in goodwill and other asset impairments (the largest component of that write-down was a $356 million goodwill write-down). While this is a non-cash expense, impairments of this magnitude are never “just accounting.” I see them as an admission that DNUT no longer believes it can earn the returns it once expected. What is particularly concerning is the breadth of the impairment. It was not limited to the US but also the UK and Australian business units, signaling that the accompanying weakness is systemic rather than isolated to a single market. Even more troubling, part of the write-down was directly tied to assets acquired to support the now-abandoned US national expansion. In simpler terms, capital allocated in this aspect is effectively wasted.

DFD Strategy Falters Amidst Weakening US Demand

Another important piece of my bull case was the DFD door expansion strategy, which I highlighted had nearly doubled in just three years previously. That growth story, however, has now flipped into something far less bullish. DNUT is, in fact, in the process of closing about 1,500 underperforming DFD doors and plans to replace them with only 1,100 new ones. So, net-net, DNUT has effectively closed 400 DFD doors. This tells us two things: (1) DNUT was wrong about the potential of 1,500 DFD doors (makes me wonder if they are expanding for the sake of expanding); and (2) DNUT is now pivoting to portfolio optimization, which speaks negatively about the underlying demand strength (why optimize now if demand is strong?).

I believe the 2Q25 numbers tell this story clearly. US organic revenue fell 3.1% y/y, which management attributed to “lower Doughnut Shop transaction volume driven by consumer softness in a challenging macroeconomic environment.” While that explanation essentially meant macro headwinds, I think the real issue is more fundamental. The way I see it now, the DFD model has never been able to replicate the unique “hot light” experience that DNUT was well-known for. As it turns out, this strategy now seems like another major misstep, as it is apparently not working. This comes at a time when consumers are pulling back on discretionary spending, which makes the overall growth outlook even worse.

Balance Sheet Under Severe Strain

All of these issues—the failed McDonald’s partnership, the massive impairments, and the failing DFD model—ultimately hit the balance sheet hard. For perspective, at the end of 2Q25, DNUT's leverage ratio under its credit facility stood at 4.5x, up from 3.9x at the close of FY24, and is very close to the 5x covenant limit. To be blunt, I actually think DNUT may have already touched this limit since it has >$134 million in “structured payables.” Once these and other obligations are included, the effective leverage profile looks even more stretched.

This weak balance sheet position severely limits what management can do to turn the business around, and it has to shift focus to deleverage the balance sheet before it can think about growth. For shareholders, this basically means “goodbye” to capital returns, which indeed, DNUT has stated it no longer expects to pay a quarterly cash dividend. DNUT is also going to lean heavily on asset sales, including refranchising certain international markets and monetizing the Western US joint venture to raise cash. For equity holders, the reality is DNUT now has a high debt load that leaves little margin for error, and there is not a clear turnaround catalyst in place.

Valuation

I don’t even know if there is a right valuation multiple to focus on for DNUT. The near-term outlook is simply too uncertain to make any convicted forecast. But per consensus estimates, earnings are going to be negative for the foreseeable future, and coupled with the fact that the balance sheet is strained, I don’t expect the market to re-rate the share price higher from here. I expect investors to stay on the sidelines and wait for tangible catalysts to appear. Until then, I think this stock will at best stay range-bound, and at worst, face further sell-down pressure.

Risk

There are upside risks. Management’s turnaround plan is focused on deleveraging and restoring profitable growth, and if executed well, it could create more near-term financial flexibility that allows it to potentially grow. At the same time, international operations are still resilient, with 2Q25 organic revenue up 5.9% y/y. The US logistics outsourcing should also provide margin relief, giving DNUT more cash flexibility.

Conclusion

In conclusion, my rating for DNUT is a sell. The failed McDonald’s partnership and DFD expansion weakness are extremely concerning. Moreover, the balance sheet is now at 4.5x leverage, making it a major overhang. Until there is a credible path to deleveraging and profitability, I believe the downside risks outweigh the potential upside.

data.title: HYBI: Not Our Cup Of Tea, Poor Macro
data.description:
Thesis

We first covered the NEOS Enhanced Income Credit Select ETF (NASDAQ:HYBI) almost a year ago, right after it re-organized from a mutual fund to a ETF under Neos. The initial article was a cursory look through the fund's composition and objectives, as well as its structuring. After almost a year of performance, data and analytics, we are going to re-visit the name and our view on this ETF given the current macro set-up.

Some analytics first

  • AUM: $0.14 bil
  • 30-day SEC yield: 6.2%
  • Distribution: monthly
  • Net expense ratio: 0.68% (there is a discount for the net to come down to 68 bps from 73 bps)

Please note the expense ratio discount is set to expire September 30, 2025 unless extended by the manager. Please note the expense ratio is very high compared to some other HY funds such as the SPDR Bloomberg High Yield Bond ETF (JNK) at 0.4%, or even the ETF's components such as the iShares Broad USD High Yield Corporate Bond ETF (USHY) where the expense ratio is 0.08%.

Fund holdings

Keeping in line with the Neos ethos, the fund holds the underlying risk factor (i.e. U.S. high yield) and then layers in put spreads on the S&P 500. Neos does this across the board with their funds, and this is their niche.

Let us look at the actual components:

The fund does not do individual credit selection, but instead opts to buy other platforms' ETFs, namely the USHY fund, the Xtrackers USD High Yield Corp Bond ETF (HYLB) and the SPDR Portfolio High Yield Bond ETF (SPHY).

The above names represent the U.S. high yield exposure, with the ETF afterwards layering in put spreads: for example the name has a Sept 11, 2025 6050/5925 put spread. This simply means that if the S&P 500 index is above the 6050 level on that date the ETF will pocket the options premium. This is the essence of put spreads. Furthermore Neos' expertise resides in structuring low delta, short maturity put spreads, which via their modelling should also result in positive results for the fund.

There is nothing wrong per se with this structuring, but there are general macro issues here relating to mixing risk factors. Generally speaking, high yield investors care about fundamentals, such as default rates, recoveries and credit spread spikes. HYBI introduces the risk of monetary loss from temporary market sell-offs from the S&P 500 as an additional risk factor. Let us now look at the fund's performance in the past year to see if investors are getting enough compensation for this additional risk.

Performance since conversion

Let us remember that the name represents a conversion, thus its history is limited:

HYBI began as the WSTCM Credit Select Risk-Managed Fund (Predecessor Fund), an open-end mutual fund which operated since September 30, 2014. As of the close of business on September 27, 2024, HYBI acquired the Predecessor Fund's assets and liabilities, and assumed its performance, financial and other historical information.

Let us run figures since October 1, 2024 then:

As we can see from the above chart, JNK is the outperformer, followed by the iShares High Yield Systematic Bond ETF (HYDB) and the Principal Active HY ETF (YLD). The Neos fund is dead last. Moreover HYBI has been lagging for a long time, specifically since the S&P 500 meltdown in April 2025.

The fund's put spread positions underperformed during April-May 2025, and dragged down performance. This is exactly the risk factor which we do not like, since defaults have not spiked, and nothing fundamentally changed for U.S. HY yield. For an active fund that charges a very high expense ratio the performance is underwhelming.

For HYBI to be successful, it needs to outperform its cohort, and ideally do it with low volatility analytics. On the Seeking Alpha 'Risk' tab the volatility figures for the name are not yet available since we have not seen a full year of data.

Macro conditions are unfavorable

In addition to a poor fund performance, we also note that the macro conditions within U.S. high yield are not favorable. High yield spreads are again very tight after spiking higher in April 2025:

When looking at the ICE BofA US High Yield Index Option-Adjusted Spread, we can see it is back at the lows of roughly 300 bps. Although not as tight as at the beginning of 2025, spreads are nonetheless very low historically.

As an investor, you want to buy into a fund like HYBI when spreads are wide, not historically tight. Even if HYBI is an active fund and benefits from a great portfolio management team, they cannot perform wonders. If we get a recession, HYBI will experience a drawdown and negative total returns. Therefore, from a macro standpoint today's environment is not a good one to buy HY (at least for retail investors). We would like to see spreads go above 4% in the above chart to consider buying the name.

Conclusion

HYBI is a fixed income exchange traded fund. The name contains building blocks in the form of other HY ETFs, and then layers in put spreads on the S&P 500 on top. The fund comes with a very high expense ratio of 68 bps, and has underperformed a cohort of peers in the past year. We are not enamored with layering in S&P 500 exposure in a HY fund, and would rather have a 'clean' take on the U.S. high yield asset class. With a stretched macro environment and a structure which has underperformed, we see no reason to own this name. We are a 'Sell' here for HYBI.

data.title: Tesla Investors Just Got Two More Reasons To Buy The Stock
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

When I last wrote about Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) in June, I said that the company’s robotaxi is worth way more than Waymo (GOOG).

Although the stock is up 7% since the time of my writing, it is up 45% since my first “buy” rating on the stock in March, outperforming the S&P 500 by over 2.5x, as can be seen below.

Meanwhile, Tesla’s board has just proposed a compensation package for its CEO, Elon Musk, that would catapult the tech entrepreneur’s wealth above $1T if the company fulfills a set of rigorous benchmarks over the next decade.

In this post, I break down the details of the proposed $1T pay package and explain why this will be a boon for Tesla’s stock.

Aside from a much more motivated and aligned Musk, the proposed package also fills the gaps between the vision Musk set with Tesla’s Master Plan IV (released last week) and translating it into tangible outcomes with the operational milestones that we discuss in the post.

In the short term, there is no doubt that Tesla’s Automotive business is struggling, though we may have seen a bottom with improvement expected in both its revenue and earnings per share from next quarter onwards, as per consensus estimates, before turning positive from Q1 FY26 onwards as a result of easing comps.

In the meantime, as investors see evidence of Musk’s vision of “sustainable abundance” translate into short-term product outcomes, their confidence will return, which will take the stock higher from current levels.

Note that Tesla’s stock is currently trading at a forward PE of just 24x its expected 2030 earnings, which I believe is abnormally cheap, especially given the rerating opportunity with improvement in its Automotive business, coupled with early traction from its Robotaxi business in 2026 and later.

As a result, I will reiterate my “buy” rating with a price target of $407 per share, representing an upside of over 16% from its current levels.

Breaking down Musk's $1T pay package (proposed)

Tesla’s board has proposed a compensation package for Musk that would catapult the tech entrepreneur’s wealth above $1T if the company fulfills a set of rigorous benchmarks over the next decade. This could increase Musk’s ownership in the company from 13.5% to 28.8% should he and Tesla be successful in fulfilling the benchmarks.

Tesla bull Dan Ives from Wedbush Securities posted on X, where he said the following:

“Tesla Board made the right decision and we are big fans on this pay package for Musk. Big goals for Musk and Tesla in this comp package as the AI Revolution takes hold and Tesla heads down the autonomous and robotics path.”

In this section, I will break down the proposed pay package so that investors are fully aligned as to what it encompasses.

Let’s begin.

The proposed pay package consists of 12 tranches, with the first tranche starting at the Tesla stock hitting a value of $2T.

With every $500B increase in market cap, Musk gets 1% of shares until the market cap hits $8.5T.

Given that Tesla’s market cap is currently $1.1T, we are looking at a roughly 8x (7.7x to be precise) increase in the value of the company, with a price of roughly $2700 per share.

If Musk is indeed successful in growing Tesla 8x along with accomplishing the operational milestone (that we discuss below), he will rake in roughly $900B over the duration of the agreement, making him the best-compensated CEO ever recorded.

Note, the full compensation would only be delivered if Musk vaults the company from its present value of $1.1T to $8.5T. With that said, here are the product milestones that Musk needs to accomplish as CEO of Tesla, which are centered on Full Self-Driving, robotaxis, robots, and the expansion of Tesla's vehicle fleet, along with achieving $400B in Adjusted EBITDA.

  1. 20M Tesla vehicle deliveries (currently there are roughly 8M Teslas)

  2. 10M Active FSD Subscriptions

  3. 1M Bots Delivered

  4. 1M Robotaxis in commercial operation

Note that each of the operational milestones must be paired with one of the market capitalization milestones for the shares underlying the next tranche to become earned shares.

These are undoubtedly some lofty operational and market cap numbers for the future, which may or may not be accomplished. But here’s why I believe that this pay package will be a boon for the stock, especially as there is a lot of short-term pessimism surrounding its Automotive business.

2 reasons why this could be a great thing for the Tesla stock

First, I believe we will be looking at a much more motivated and aligned Musk, assuming the proposed $1T pay package is approved.

This will be a relief for Tesla shareholders, especially as Musk/DOGE distractions from earlier this year, along with his frequent feuds with President Donald Trump, have created a black cloud over Tesla’s stock.

Second, the proposed pay package is also timely, given that Musk had released Tesla’s Master Plan IV just last week, where he made it clear that the next chapter in Tesla’s story is where the company will combine their verticalized manufacturing capabilities and autonomous prowess to deliver new products and services that will bring AI into the physical world.

In Tesla’s Master Plan IV, Musk acknowledges the challenges ahead, yet asserts that it has the necessary tools and expertise to take the next great leap towards “sustainable abundance” that is built around labor (Optimus), mobility (autonomy), and energy (Powerwall and Megapack), all tied together with AI.

In fact, shortly after releasing Tesla’s Master Plan IV, Musk posted on X , where he said that “~80% of Tesla’s value will be Optimus.”

While he has not provided a timeline to his bold claim above, I believe the proposed $1T pay package does the job in filling the gaps between the vision Musk set with Tesla’s Master Plan IV and translating it into tangible outcomes with the operational milestones that we discussed in the previous section.

The way that I see it, as investors see evidence of Musk’s vision of “sustainable abundance” translate into short-term product outcomes, their confidence will return, which will take the stock higher from current levels.

Touching base on Tesla’s Robotaxi and Optimus endeavors

I have written extensively on Tesla’s robotaxi in my previous posts.

While it faces growing competition from Waymo and others , I continue to reiterate my bullish stance on Tesla’s robotaxi, as it was able to reach its milestone of growing its Bay Area robotaxi fleet from 10 to 100 in August, while rolling out its robotaxi app to the general public, signaling plans to broaden the service beyond a limited group of early access users in Austin, Texas.

While these milestones may seem miniscule, let’s not forget that Tesla’s main advantage is tied to its vertically integrated technology that consists of energy, autonomy, and compute, enabling it to produce cars at 25% of what a Waymo costs.

Not only that, but Tesla’s data advantage is also significant, and the more cars it puts on the road, the more data it has to train its AI models, thus enabling it to accelerate its FSD capabilities with increasing parameters.

Plus, there have been a growing number of reports and influencer comparisons in the last couple of months available on social media. William Blair and MKBHD are some notable reviews comparing Tesla's Robotaxi to Google's Waymo and Uber.

While I won’t go into the technological differences amongst these companies (something I have covered in my previous posts before), I will highlight the following William Blair research note, which aligns strongly with what I have been talking about in my previous posts on Tesla’s robotaxi.

“Robotaxi pricing was consistently half that of Uber, confirming our thesis that Tesla could win market share by leveraging its lower cost structure. We estimate Tesla’s autonomous tech stack is one-tenth the cost of Waymo, with significant manufacturing scale and cost optimization multiple years ahead. In our robotaxi model, we forecast Tesla to continue to undercut price by 50% and achieve the target of $0.30 cost per mile in the 2030s as the dedicated robotaxi vehicle scales.

Tesla has the ability to leverage its lower cost structure and weaponize pricing—charging 50% less per mile, it can still achieve near 60% EBITDA margins. We expect Tesla to win 35% market share versus competitors Waymo at 15%, Uber at 38%, and Lyft at 13%, generating almost $250 billion in revenue in 2040.”

When it comes to revenue projections from Robotaxi in the next five years, estimates range from $75B to $100B. If we attach a price-to-sales ratio of 10x, we will arrive at an incremental market cap of $850B+ to $1T (optimistic) in the next 5 years (over the company’s existing $1.1T in market cap), bringing it to a total of $2T+, taking into account its existing business lines and robotaxi.

Shifting our attention to Tesla’s Optimus (bots) unit, Musk said that he expects prototypes of Optimus 3 by the end of this year and scaled production next year, with the possibility that the company could produce 1M robots a year by 2030.

Plus, earlier he had also suggested the robots are expected to cost between $20,000 and $30,000 per unit.

While it is hard to speculate on the actual number of Optimus bots that will be sold by 2030 depending on adoption curves across industries along with variability in pricing, I would like to point out that Morgan Stanley estimates that the humanoid market could surpass $5T by 2050.

In a slightly shorter time frame, Goldman Sachs estimates that the global market for humanoid robots could reach $38B by 2035, especially as robot components become more affordable.

So, if we have to stitch these two reports together, we are likely looking at the humanoid robot market growing at a CAGR of 25%+ between 2030 and 2050. Note that these numbers are hugely speculative and therefore subject to revisions.

But, at a high level, I can say that we are likely to see adoption curves for humanoid robots start to accelerate from 2030 onwards from growing reliability and advanced AI. Therefore, a CAGR of 25%+ growth doesn’t sound unfeasible to me.

Second, I strongly believe that Tesla’s Optimus will command a dominating share in the robotics market given the company’s data advantage and verticalized manufacturing capabilities, allowing it to scale production much more efficiently than any other competitor in the market at the moment.

The auto business is still hurting, but a PE of 24x 2030 earnings is abnormally cheap.

In the short term, there is no doubt that its core Automotive business continued to struggle in Q2 FY25, where revenues declined -16% YoY to $16.6B for the segment due to a decline in vehicle deliveries and reduced ASPs (Average Selling Price). At the same time, total revenues for the company declined at a faster rate compared to the previous quarter at -12% YoY to $22.5B, driven by a decline in Energy Generation and Storage Revenue from lower ASPs.

Yet, despite the faster decline in revenues on a sequential basis, Tesla managed to grow their gross and operating margins to 17.2% and 4.1%, respectively, as a result of lower cost per vehicle and growth in Energy Generation & Storage profits.

Moving forward, we should see both its revenue and earnings per share start to tick higher before turning positive from Q1 FY26 onwards as a result of easing comps.

Taking the current consensus estimates for both revenue and earnings into account, here’s my estimate for Tesla’s price target.

Starting with revenue, Tesla is expected to see its revenues grow in the 20%+ range until FY30, where I believe robotaxis will play a contributing factor. This will translate to a total revenue of $269B by FY30.

From a profitability standpoint, the company's earnings per share are expected to grow at a much faster rate than its revenues as a result of expanding operating leverage from its vertically integrated manufacturing capabilities that will help it first scale its robotaxi/cybercab business, followed by Optimus. This will result in Tesla earning $13.94 per share in FY30, which is equivalent to a present value of $9.06 per share when discounted at 9%.

Currently, Tesla is trading at a forward price-to-earnings multiple of just 24x its projected earnings in FY30. I believe that is severely undervalued, especially given the growth trajectories associated with the company’s robotaxi and Optimus business lines, which investors continue to discount as they focus on the immediate short-term pressures from the company’s Automotive segment.

I believe that Tesla deserves a premium of at least 2.5 times the average S&P 500 price-to-earnings multiple given its projected earnings growth trajectory. This will translate to a forward PE of 45, or a price target of $407 per share, which represents an upside of 16% from current levels.

My final verdict and conclusion

With a forward price-to-earnings multiple of 24x its FY30 earnings, I believe that the revenue risk from its Robotaxi and Optimus business lines (which are pre-revenue) is fully accounted for.

In the meantime, I believe that the proposed $1T CEO pay package will be a boon for Tesla and its shareholders, as we will have a much more motivated and aligned Musk, with a clear line of sight into product outcomes to fulfill his vision from Tesla’s Master Plan IV.

Whether the market value of Tesla indeed grows 8x from current levels is irrelevant at the moment. What is required is for investor confidence to return back, and I believe it will happen as we see improvement in its Automotive business, along with traction in its Robotaxi business from 2026 onwards, with Optimus to follow shortly after that.

As a result, I will continue to reiterate my “buy” rating with a price target of $407 per share, representing an upside of 16% from its current levels.

data.title: Trilogy Metals: Congress Removes Impediments To Ambler Access Road
data.description:
Trilogy Metals (TMQ)(TMQ:CA) received a boost with the House vote to overturn the Central Yukon Resource Management Plan. The Senate is expected to consider its version of the House resolution by mid-October, and that is very likely to pass as well.

That would remove a key obstacle to the Ambler Access Road. South32 has also indicated that there has been strong progress in dialogues with Alaskan Native groups, although there is still work to do there.

The potential value of Trilogy's assets appears to be improving over time with rising metals prices plus the US government's interest in securing domestic production.

Balanced against that is the likely probability that it will still take a while for the Ambler Access Road to be built, and I am currently assuming that Trilogy's Arctic Project won't start producing until the mid-2030s.

I have increased my estimate of Trilogy's intrinsic value from US$1.40 to US$1.75 (around CAD2.42) per share, and believe it is roughly fairly valued currently.

This report uses US dollars as the main currency, along with an exchange rate of US$1.00 to CAD1.38 if needed.

Congressional Votes

The House recently passed a resolution to overturn the Central Yukon Resource Management Plan. This plan was implemented under the Biden administration and limited development on a large amount of federal lands in Alaska. This would have effectively prevented the Ambler Access Road since the road needs to cross the relevant federal land.

The House vote was 215 to 210 in favor of the resolution, and it was passed nearly fully along party lines. The Senate version of the resolution is expected to be considered by mid-October. With the Republicans holding a 53-47 majority in the Senate, that resolution is very likely to pass as well. Thus the situation around federal approval looks quite good.

Some Remaining Challenges

Although the regulatory situation around the Ambler Access Road looks quite positive, it will still likely require a fair amount of time to get the Arctic Project built and into production. Senator Murkowski commented that there are still obstacles to the Ambler Access Project despite the Congressional resolutions.

Alaskan Native support will be critical. South32 (the JV partner for Ambler Metals) mentioned that there's been a lot of work over the last several years to build relations with various Alaska Native groups, and that it believes it is close to getting strong support from those groups.

South32 also views Ambler Metals as "a good medium-to-long-term prospect." It also needs to do more exploration work to fully understand the potential of the area, with seasonal weather constraints limiting the time for exploration.

The medium-to-long-term comment reflects that the process is still likely to take a while even with the federal support. Legal challenges could slow down road building activities, and the Ambler Access Road itself is a major and lengthy undertaking. As a result, I believe that 2035 or so may be a reasonable time to expect Arctic Project production to begin, and that would probably require a Republican presidential victory in 2028 as well to avoid the potential for regulatory setbacks.

It appears that the federal government is transferring federal lands in Alaska to state control (and to Alaskan native corporations), reducing some of the ability of the federal government to restrict development though.

Improving Economics

Rising metal prices have improved Arctic's valuation at spot metal prices. At the beginning of September 2025 prices, the after-tax NPV8 is US$2.288 billion, which is a 106% increase compared to what it would be with base case metal prices.

As well, this is a 28% increase compared to the after-tax NPV8 based on spot metal prices from August 2024.

Current spot prices may end up differing greatly from the actual metal prices at the time when Arctic is able to start producing. I believe that the base case metal prices are quite conservative though.

Notes On Valuation

With the progress around federal approval and improved spot prices, I now estimate Trilogy's value at US$1.75 per share (up from US$1.40 per share).

Trilogy currently has 164 million outstanding shares. When RSUs, DSUs and exercisable options (most of which are now in the money) are included, Trilogy would have approximately 179 million shares.

At 179 million shares, a valuation of US$1.75 per share would give Trilogy a market cap of around US$313 million.

I believe this is reasonable given the potentially long time before Arctic is built and starts generating cash flow. I am also factoring in Trilogy's potential need to issue more shares for funding.

Trilogy had US$24.6 million in cash at the end of May 2025, and its full fiscal 2025 cash budget was US$3.1 million. It has plenty of money corporately, but will need to fund the Ambler Metals JV soon, as Ambler Metals has enough funding to last until early 2026 currently.

Trilogy has a Base Shelf Prospectus that allows for the future issuance of up to US$50 million in securities. It also has established an ATM program (not utilized yet as of July 10, 2025) that allows it to sell up to US$25 million in common shares.

Full exercise of the outstanding exercisable options may give Trilogy around US$11 million in additional funds.

Conclusion

Congress is moving to overturn the Central Yukon Resource Management Plan, with the House passing its resolution and the Senate likely to pass its resolution later this year. This would remove a key obstacle to the Ambler Access Road.

While the Congressional action is a significant positive, there is still a lengthy path ahead to get the Ambler Access Road built and then the Arctic Project built. I am assuming that the Arctic Project can start producing in 2035, which may even be on the optimistic side.

data.title: Gold ETF Flows: August 2025
data.description:
August in review

Global physically backed gold ETFs1 attracted US$5.5bn in August, extending their inflow streak to three months (Chart 1).2 Similar to July, North American and European funds led global inflows while Asia and other regions saw mild outflows (Table 1). Nevertheless, the y-t-d inflow of US$47bn reached the second strongest on record after the peak of 2020.

August inflows and a further rise in the gold price pushed global gold ETFs’ total assets under management (AUM) 5% higher to US$407bn, setting a new month-end record. Holdings continued to increase, rising 53t to 3,692t, the highest month-end value since July 2022 and 6% below the record of 3,929t, which was reached the first week of November 2020.

Regional overview

North American funds added US$4.1bn in August, the region’s third consecutive monthly inflow.

Continued strength in demand can be linked to:

  • Persistent trade risk and broader market uncertainty
  • The consensus short dollar trade, which reduces the opportunity cost of holding gold
  • Lower rate expectations as the market digested Powell’s Jackson Hole comments as dovish.

The latter was arguably the most important catalyst into month-end. Outflows that had been seen in the days leading up to Jackson Hole reversed swiftly, as investors anticipated a September rate cut.

It is also notable that low-cost gold backed ETFs, often viewed as a proxy for long-term strategic positioning, are having their best year on record (Chart 2). We consider this to be a signal that – beyond short-term market noise – investors are steadily building safe-haven allocations in response to a backdrop of elevated risks.

European funds have now experienced inflows four months in a row, adding US$1.9bn in August. The UK, Switzerland, and Germany led the charge. During the month, the US imposed a surprise 39% tariff on Switzerland, the highest on any developed nation.3 This sudden and unexpected hit has affected the country’s economic prospects, pushed up safe-haven needs among local investors and increased demand for gold.

German inflows may have also been supported by higher safe-haven demand as the country’s Q2 GDP growth was revised down further, sparking investor fears of recession.4 Meanwhile, with the euro and Swiss franc strengthening against the dollar, holdings in FX-hedged products also rose.

The UK also witnessed strong inflows in the month, likely buoyed by stagflation concerns – the country’s inflation rebounded while the US tariffs and a tax hike on employers – which could also push up prices further – cloud growth.

Asian flows flipped negative in August, losing US$495mn. China lost the most: continued equity strength, with the CSI300 Stock Index jumping 10% in August, kept diverting local investors away from gold. In contrast, India saw its fourth consecutive monthly inflow in August, supported by elevated safe-haven needs amid weak equities as well as ongoing global trade and geopolitical risks. But they were insufficient to offset Chinese outflows. Flows in other regions remained mildly negative, shedding US$50mn. Australian inflows were insufficient to offset South African outflows in the month.

Volumes remain stable

Gold market trading volumes remained broadly unchanged, averaging US$290bn per day – just 2% lower m/m.5 The minor decline was led by a 17% m/m drop in exchange-traded volumes – trading at both COMEX and Shanghai Futures Exchange cooled. Despite this, the average volume of US$114bn/day has remained above its 2024 level of US$102bn/day. Gold ETF trading volumes were also down across all regions, decreasing 9% m/m to US$4.5bn/day. But OTC trading activities rose, reaching US$171bn/day on average in the month, 12% higher than July and well above the 2024 average of US$128bn/day.

Total net longs in COMEX gold futures fell 3.4% during the month, concluding August at 652t,6 while money manager net longs rose 3.7% to 461t. Money managers’ bullish bets rose steeply in early August around the news of US tariffs on Swiss bullion, leading to a surge in the COMEX gold price. Consecutive price rises towards the end of the month also saw gold futures traders build up their longs.

*We monitor how fund assets change through time by looking at two key metrics: demand and fund flows.

  • Gold ETF demand is the change in gold holdings during a given period. We use this metric to calculate the quarterly demand estimates reported in Gold Demand Trends.

  • Fund flows represent the amount of money – reported in US dollars – that investors have put into (or retrieved from) a fund during a given period. For more details, see our methodology note.

† 'Global Inflows/positive demand’ refers to the sum of changes of all funds that saw a net increase in holdings over a given period (e.g., month, quarter, etc.). Conversely, ‘global outflows/negative demand’ aggregates changes from funds that saw holdings decline over the same period.

Footnotes

  1. We define gold ETFs as regulated securities that hold gold in physical form. These include open-ended funds traded on regulated exchanges and other regulated products such as closed-end funds and mutual funds. A complete list is included in the gold ETF section of Goldhub.com.

  2. We track gold ETF assets in two ways: the quantity of gold they hold, generally measured in tonnes, and the equivalent value of those holdings in US dollars (AUM). We also monitor how these fund assets change through time by looking at two key metrics: demand and fund flows. For more detail, see our ETF methodology note.

  3. See: Trump tariffs: Why Switzerland faces a unique struggle, 7 August 2025.

  4. See: Germany falls back into ‘recessionary territory’ as second-quarter GDP revised down, 22 August 2025.

  5. Due to LBMA trading volume data availability, our full trading volume dataset dates back to 2018.

  6. Based on CFTC positioning report as of 26 August 2025.
